title: Authentication
content: Access to the docs site is restricted. Only authenticated users with an allowed email address can view content. Authentication is handled by NextAuth.js and supports two OAuth providers: Azure AD and Google.

title: How It Works
content: When a user visits any docs page, the middleware checks for a valid session. Unauthenticated users are redirected to the login page at /[locale]/auth/login. After signing in through Azure AD or Google, the user's email is checked against the allow-list before access is granted.

title: Internal users
content: Any email ending in @tcc-technology.com is granted access automatically — no additional configuration required.

title: External users
content: Add external email addresses to the ALLOWED_EXTERNAL_EMAILS environment variable as a comma-separated list.

title: External users
content: Without an expiry date:

title: External users
content: With an expiry date (YYYY-MM-DD):

title: External users
content: When an expiry date is reached, the user's session cookie is deleted on their next request — they are signed out automatically without any manual intervention.

title: External users
content: The expiry date 2026-12-31 takes effect at 00:00 UTC on that day. To allow access through the end of that calendar day, use 2027-01-01 instead.

title: Environment Variables
content: All environment variables must be set before starting the server. Copy .env.example to .env.local and fill in the values.

title: Environment Variables
content: | Variable | Required | Description |

title: Environment Variables
content: | AZURE_AD_CLIENT_ID | Yes | Azure AD application (client) ID |

title: Environment Variables
content: | AZURE_AD_CLIENT_SECRET | Yes | Azure AD client secret |

title: Environment Variables
content: | AZURE_AD_TENANT_ID | Yes | Azure AD tenant ID |

title: Environment Variables
content: | GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID | Yes | Google OAuth client ID |

title: Environment Variables
content: | GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET | Yes | Google OAuth client secret |

title: Environment Variables
content: | NEXTAUTH_SECRET | Yes | Secret used to sign and verify session tokens |

title: Environment Variables
content: | NEXTAUTH_URL | Yes | Canonical URL of the site (e.g. https://docs.example.com) |

title: Environment Variables
content: | ALLOWED_EXTERNAL_EMAILS | No | Comma-separated external emails, each optionally followed by :YYYY-MM-DD |

id: Authentication > Setting Up OAuth Providers > Azure AD#l0-i0
title: Azure AD
content: Go to Azure Portal → Azure Active Directory → App registrations → New registration.

id: Authentication > Setting Up OAuth Providers > Azure AD#l0-i1
title: Azure AD
content: Set the redirect URI to https://your-domain.com/api/auth/callback/azure-ad.

id: Authentication > Setting Up OAuth Providers > Azure AD#l0-i2
title: Azure AD
content: Create a client secret under Certificates & secrets.

id: Authentication > Setting Up OAuth Providers > Azure AD#l0-i3
title: Azure AD
content: Copy the Application (client) ID, Client secret, and Directory (tenant) ID into your .env.local.

id: Authentication > Setting Up OAuth Providers > Google#l0-i0
title: Google
content: Go to Google Cloud Console → APIs & Services → Credentials → Create credentials → OAuth client ID.

id: Authentication > Setting Up OAuth Providers > Google#l0-i1
title: Google
content: Set the authorized redirect URI to https://your-domain.com/api/auth/callback/google.

id: Authentication > Setting Up OAuth Providers > Google#l0-i2
title: Google
content: Copy the Client ID and Client secret into your .env.local.

id: Authentication > Setting Up OAuth Providers > Google#p1
title: Google
content: For local development, add http://localhost:4000/api/auth/callback/azure-ad and http://localhost:4000/api/auth/callback/google as additional redirect URIs in each provider's settings.

title: Generating NEXTAUTH_SECRET
content: Generate a secure random secret with:

title: Generating NEXTAUTH_SECRET
content: Paste the output as the value of NEXTAUTH_SECRET in .env.local.

title: Generating NEXTAUTH_SECRET
content: Do not commit .env.local to version control. NEXTAUTH_SECRET must remain private — anyone with this value can forge session tokens.

title: Customization
content: This page covers the most common ways to adapt docs-template to your project: site metadata, logo, theme colors, navigation order, and adding new locales.

title: Site Metadata
content: Edit src/app/layout.tsx to update the global <title>, <meta name="description">, and other head tags that apply to every page.

title: Logo
content: The docs header logo is rendered by src/components/docs/docs-logo.tsx. Replace the contents of that component with your own image or SVG:

title: Logo
content: Place your logo file in the public/ directory so it is served as a static asset.

title: Theme Colors
content: docs-template uses Tailwind CSS v4, which uses CSS custom properties for design tokens. Update the variables in src/styles/globals.css to change brand colors, background, and foreground tokens for both light and dark modes:

title: Theme Colors
content: Tailwind CSS v4 recommends oklch for perceptually uniform colors. Adjust the lightness, chroma, and hue values to match your brand.

title: Navigation Structure
content: The sidebar order matches the order of entries in each locale's navigation.json. Reorder entries to change the sidebar order:

title: Navigation Structure
content: Update both src/docs/en/navigation.json and src/docs/th/navigation.json to keep the two locales in sync.

id: Customization > Adding a New Locale > 1. Register the locale#p0
title: 1. Register the locale
content: Add the new locale to src/i18n/routing.ts:

id: Customization > Adding a New Locale > 2. Add UI strings#p0
title: 2. Add UI strings
content: Create messages/[locale].json with all the same keys as messages/en.json, translated into the new language.

id: Customization > Adding a New Locale > 3. Create docs content#p0
title: 3. Create docs content
content: Create a docs folder at src/docs/[locale]/ containing:

id: Customization > Adding a New Locale > 3. Create docs content#l1-i0
title: 3. Create docs content
content: navigation.json — sidebar structure (translated titles)

id: Customization > Adding a New Locale > 3. Create docs content#l1-i1
title: 3. Create docs content
content: [group]/[slug].mdx — MDX content files for each page

id: Customization > Adding a New Locale > 3. Create docs content#p2
title: 3. Create docs content
content: Each locale's navigation.json must contain the same group and page slugs. Mismatched slugs will result in broken links or 404 pages when switching locales.

title: Deployment
content: docs-template can be deployed to any platform that supports Next.js. This page covers the three most common deployment targets: Vercel, Netlify, and a self-hosted Node.js server.

title: Before You Deploy
content: Make sure the following are in place before deploying:

title: Before You Deploy
content: All required environment variables are configured in your deployment platform.

title: Before You Deploy
content: OAuth redirect URIs for Azure AD and Google are updated to point to your production domain.

title: Before You Deploy
content: After deploying, add https://your-domain.com/api/auth/callback/azure-ad and https://your-domain.com/api/auth/callback/google as authorized redirect URIs in your OAuth provider settings. Authentication will fail without this step.

title: Vercel
content: Vercel is the recommended deployment platform for Next.js projects.

title: Vercel
content: Push your repository to GitHub, GitLab, or Bitbucket.

title: Vercel
content: Go to vercel.com and click Add New Project.

title: Vercel
content: Import the repository.

title: Vercel
content: Add all required environment variables under Environment Variables in the project settings.

title: Vercel
content: Click Deploy.

title: Vercel
content: Vercel handles builds, previews, and zero-downtime deployments automatically.

title: Netlify
content: Push your repository to a Git provider.

title: Netlify
content: Go to netlify.com and click Add new site → Import an existing project.

title: Netlify
content: Import the repository.

title: Netlify
content: Set the Build command to bun run generate-git-metadata && bun run build and the Publish directory to .next.

title: Netlify
content: Install the Next.js on Netlify build plugin if it is not added automatically.

title: Netlify
content: Add all required environment variables under Site configuration → Environment variables.

title: Netlify
content: Click Deploy site.

title: Automated Releases (CI)
content: This template includes a GitHub Actions workflow (.github/workflows/release.yml) that runs semantic-release on every push to main. It automatically bumps the version, generates a changelog, and creates a GitHub Release.

title: GH_TOKEN
content: The workflow requires a repository secret named GH_TOKEN — a Personal Access Token (PAT) that gives semantic-release permission to:

title: GH_TOKEN
content: Create and update GitHub Releases

title: GH_TOKEN
content: Push the release commit back to the repo (updated CHANGELOG.md, package.json, bun.lock)

title: GH_TOKEN
content: It is stored as a secret rather than using the default GITHUB_TOKEN because semantic-release needs to push commits that can trigger other workflows — the built-in token intentionally cannot do that.

id: Deployment > Automated Releases (CI) > GH_TOKEN > Generating the token#p0
title: Generating the token
content: Fine-grained token (recommended):

id: Deployment > Automated Releases (CI) > GH_TOKEN > Generating the token#l1-i0
title: Generating the token
content: Go to GitHub → Settings → Developer settings → Personal access tokens → Fine-grained tokens

id: Deployment > Automated Releases (CI) > GH_TOKEN > Generating the token#l1-i1
title: Generating the token
content: Click Generate new token, set an expiration and resource owner

id: Deployment > Automated Releases (CI) > GH_TOKEN > Generating the token#l1-i2
title: Generating the token
content: Under Repository permissions, grant:

id: Deployment > Automated Releases (CI) > GH_TOKEN > Generating the token#l1-i3
title: Generating the token
content: Contents: Read and write

id: Deployment > Automated Releases (CI) > GH_TOKEN > Generating the token#l1-i4
title: Generating the token
content: Issues: Read and write

id: Deployment > Automated Releases (CI) > GH_TOKEN > Generating the token#l1-i5
title: Generating the token
content: Pull requests: Read and write

id: Deployment > Automated Releases (CI) > GH_TOKEN > Generating the token#l1-i6
title: Generating the token
content: Metadata: Read-only (auto-selected)

id: Deployment > Automated Releases (CI) > GH_TOKEN > Generating the token#l1-i7
title: Generating the token
content: Click Generate token and copy it

id: Deployment > Automated Releases (CI) > GH_TOKEN > Generating the token#p2
title: Generating the token
content: Classic token:

id: Deployment > Automated Releases (CI) > GH_TOKEN > Generating the token#p3
title: Generating the token
content: Select the repo scope — that covers all the permissions semantic-release needs.

id: Deployment > Automated Releases (CI) > GH_TOKEN > Adding the secret#l0-i0
title: Adding the secret
content: Go to your repo → Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions → New repository secret

id: Deployment > Automated Releases (CI) > GH_TOKEN > Adding the secret#l0-i1
title: Adding the secret
content: Name: GH_TOKEN

id: Deployment > Automated Releases (CI) > GH_TOKEN > Adding the secret#l0-i2
title: Adding the secret
content: Value: paste the token you generated

id: Deployment > Automated Releases (CI) > GH_TOKEN > Adding the secret#p1
title: Adding the secret
content: Secrets are never copied when a new repository is created from a template. Each repository must add its own GH_TOKEN secret for the release workflow to function.

title: Installation
content: This guide walks you through cloning the repository, installing dependencies, and running the development server locally.

title: Prerequisites
content: Before you begin, make sure you have the following installed:

title: Prerequisites
content: Node.js 20+ — Download

title: Prerequisites
content: Bun (recommended) — Download

title: Prerequisites
content: The project uses Bun as its package manager and script runner. While npm or pnpm may work, all scripts and lockfiles are optimized for Bun.

title: 1. Clone the repository
content: Alternatively, click "Use this template" on the repository page on GitHub to create a new repository from this template.

title: 1. Clone the repository
content: This repository is maintained exclusively for TCC Technology internal projects. Do not use it for public or third-party projects.

title: 3. Configure environment variables
content: Copy the example environment file and fill in the required values:

title: 3. Configure environment variables
content: See the Authentication page for a full description of each environment variable.

id: Installation > Setup > 4. Start the development server#p0
title: 4. Start the development server
content: Open http://localhost:4000 in your browser. The bun dev command automatically generates the full-text search index before starting the server.

title: Available Scripts
content: | Script | Description |

title: Available Scripts
content: | bun dev | Generate search index, then start the development server on port 4000 |

title: Available Scripts
content: | bun build | Generate search index, then build for production |

title: Available Scripts
content: | bun start | Start the production server on port 4000 |

title: Available Scripts
content: | bun lint | Run ESLint across the project |

title: Available Scripts
content: | bun run generate-search-index | Build the full-text search index from MDX content |

title: Available Scripts
content: | bun run generate-git-metadata | Pre-generate Git last-modified metadata for all doc pages |

title: Pre-build Scripts
content: Two scripts prepare static assets that the site depends on. Both are called automatically by bun dev and bun build, but you can also run them manually.

title: Search index
content: Scans all MDX files and writes JSON search indexes to public/:

title: Git metadata
content: Reads git log to collect last-modified dates and authors for every doc page. The output is written to public/git-metadata.json:

title: Git metadata
content: generate-git-metadata is wired to lint-staged and runs automatically on every git commit, so the metadata file stays current without any manual steps.

title: Introduction
content: docs-template is a documentation site template built with Next.js, MDX, and Tailwind CSS. Write your docs in MDX files, configure navigation with a single JSON file, and get a fully-featured docs site with syntax highlighting, full-text search, table of contents, dark mode, internationalization, and more — out of the box.

title: Introduction
content: This template is maintained exclusively for TCC Technology internal projects and is not intended for public or third-party use.

title: Features
content: The following capabilities are included with no additional configuration required:

title: Features
content: MDX-based content — Write documentation in Markdown with JSX component support

title: Features
content: Internationalization (i18n) — Multi-locale support powered by next-intl, with per-locale MDX content and navigation

title: Features
content: File-based routing — Pages map directly to src/docs/[locale]/[group]/[slug].mdx

title: Features
content: JSON-driven navigation — Define sidebar structure in src/docs/[locale]/navigation.json

title: Features
content: Syntax highlighting — Powered by Shiki with copy-to-clipboard support

title: Features
content: Full-text search — Client-side fuzzy search using Fuse.js

title: Features
content: Table of contents — Auto-generated from headings in each MDX page

title: Features
content: Dark / light mode — Theme toggling with system preference detection

title: Features
content: Git metadata — Last modified date and author pulled from Git history

title: Features
content: Callouts — Info, warning, and danger callout blocks

title: Features
content: Responsive layout — Sidebar drawer for mobile, sticky sidebar for desktop

title: Tech Stack
content: | Area | Library |

title: Tech Stack
content: | Framework | Next.js 16 (App Router) |

title: Tech Stack
content: | Styling | Tailwind CSS v4 |

title: Tech Stack
content: | MDX | @next/mdx, @mdx-js/react |

title: Tech Stack
content: | i18n | next-intl |

title: Tech Stack
content: | Syntax highlighting | Shiki |

title: Tech Stack
content: | Search | Fuse.js |

title: Tech Stack
content: | Animations | Framer Motion |

title: Tech Stack
content: | Icons | Remixicon |

title: Tech Stack
content: | State management | Zustand |

title: Tech Stack
content: | Components | Radix UI |

title: Project Structure
content: The source code is organized under src/ as follows:

title: Next Steps
content: Continue with the Installation guide to set up the project locally, or jump to Writing Docs if you already have a running instance.

title: Writing Docs
content: Documentation pages are MDX files stored under src/docs/[locale]/[group]/[slug].mdx. This page explains how to create a page, register it in navigation, and use the available components.

id: Writing Docs > Adding a New Page > 1. Create the MDX file#p0
title: 1. Create the MDX file
content: Create a file at src/docs/[locale]/[group]/[slug].mdx for each locale. Every file must start with a metadata export:

id: Writing Docs > Adding a New Page > 2. Register in navigation#p0
title: 2. Register in navigation
content: Add the page to src/docs/en/navigation.json and src/docs/th/navigation.json:

id: Writing Docs > Adding a New Page > 2. Register in navigation#p1
title: 2. Register in navigation
content: The page will be available at /en/docs/my-group/my-page and /th/docs/my-group/my-page.

id: Writing Docs > Adding a New Group#p0
title: Adding a New Group
content: Append a new entry to the top-level array in both navigation files:

id: Writing Docs > Adding a New Group#p1
title: Adding a New Group
content: The sidebar order matches the order of entries in navigation.json. Reorder entries to change the sidebar order.

id: Writing Docs > Using Components > Auto-mapped elements#p0
title: Auto-mapped elements
content: The following elements are auto-mapped to styled components — no import needed:

id: Writing Docs > Using Components > Auto-mapped elements#p1
title: Auto-mapped elements
content: | Element | Rendered as |

id: Writing Docs > Using Components > Auto-mapped elements#p1
title: Auto-mapped elements

id: Writing Docs > Using Components > Auto-mapped elements#p1
title: Auto-mapped elements
content: | Headings (#, ##, ###) | Styled headings with anchor links |

id: Writing Docs > Using Components > Auto-mapped elements#p1

content: | Paragraphs | Styled body text |

id: Writing Docs > Using Components > Auto-mapped elements#p1

content: | Lists (-, 1.) | Styled bullet and ordered lists |

id: Writing Docs > Using Components > Auto-mapped elements#p1

content: | Tables | Styled responsive tables |

id: Writing Docs > Using Components > Auto-mapped elements#p1

id: Writing Docs > Using Components > Auto-mapped elements#p1

id: Writing Docs > Using Components > Auto-mapped elements#p1

title: Callout
content: Use Callout to draw attention to important information. Import it at the top of your MDX file:

title: Callout
content: Then use it in your content:

title: Callout
content: Available type values:

title: Callout
content: | Type | Use case |

title: Callout
content: | info | Setup notes, prerequisites, context |

title: Callout
content: | tip | Best practices, shortcuts |

title: Callout
content: | warning | Potential issues, gotchas |

title: Callout
content: | danger | Destructive or irreversible actions |

title: Callout
content: Add isCollapsed to render the callout collapsed by default:

title: Code Blocks
content: Use fenced code blocks with the language identifier on the opening fence.

id: Writing Docs > Code Blocks > Copy button#p0
title: Copy button
content: Add copy="true" to show a copy-to-clipboard button:

id: Writing Docs > Code Blocks > File path label#p0
title: File path label
content: Append :path/to/file after the language identifier to display a filename header above the block:

id: Writing Docs > Code Blocks > File path label#p1
title: File path label
content: The text after : is rendered as a label. It does not affect syntax highlighting.

id: Writing Docs > Code Blocks > File path label#p2
title: File path label
content: Syntax highlighting is powered by Shiki. Any language Shiki supports can be used as the language identifier.
